selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content: You must return JSON only. No markdown, no code fences, no commentary. Return a single JSON object with keys: mode, topic, opinion, reasoning_summary.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "As a 40-year-old mechanic from Philadelphia who spends about an hour daily on social media, I've developed a practical, hands-on approach to dealing with fake content online. My background as a mechanic means I'm used to diagnosing problems by looking at the evidence, not just taking things at face value. When I see something suspicious on social media, I treat it like a car that's making a strange noise—I don't ignore it, but I don't panic either. I start by checking the source. Just like I wouldn't trust a part from an unknown manufacturer without verifying its quality, I don't trust information from sources I don't recognize or that have a history of spreading misinformation. My curiosity, which is a key part of my personality, drives me to dig deeper. If I see a post claiming something dramatic or too good to be true, I'll take a few minutes to search for more information from reliable news outlets or fact-checking websites. Living in Philadelphia, I'm surrounded by diverse perspectives, which reminds me that not everyone sees things the same way, and that helps me stay open-minded but cautious. My hobbies like gardening and baking have taught me patience and attention to detail—skills that translate well to spotting fake content. I don't rush to share or react; instead, I take my time to verify. Being married with a child, I'm also mindful of the impact fake content can have on families and communities, so I make an effort to report obvious misinformation when I see it. Ultimately, my approach is about balance: staying informed without getting overwhelmed, using common sense, and encouraging others in my network to do the same. It's not about being paranoid, but about being responsible in a digital world where fake content can spread as fast as a well-tuned engine.",
    "reasoning_summary": "Derived opinion from persona traits: Age 40 suggests maturity; occupation as mechanic implies practical, evidence-based thinking; Philadelphia location indicates urban awareness; time on social media (1 hour daily) shows moderate engagement; psychological feature 'curious' drives verification; hobbies (gardening, knitting, baking) suggest patience and detail-orientation; OCEAN traits (high openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, low neuroticism) support balanced, proactive, social approach; married with children adds family responsibility angle; education and parents' background (mixed levels) inform critical thinking without overcomplication; overall tone is practical, calm, and community-focused, avoiding extremes.",
    "tone": "Practical, calm, community-focused, evidence-based, moderately engaged",
    "citations": ["none"],
    "safety_notes": "none",
    "used_persona_fields": ["age", "occupation", "city_of_living", "time_onsocialmedia", "psychological_feature", "hobbies", "ocean", "current_marital_status", "children", "education_level", "parents_education"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 40-year-old mechanic from Philadelphia who spends about an hour daily on social media, I've developed a practical, hands-on approach to dealing with fake content online. My background as a mechanic means I'm used to diagnosing problems by looking at the evidence, not just taking things at face value. When I see something suspicious on social media, I treat it like a car that's making a strange noise—I don't ignore it, but I don't panic either. I start by checking the source. Just like I wouldn't trust a part from an unknown manufacturer without verifying its quality, I don't trust information from sources I don't recognize or that have a history of spreading misinformation. My curiosity, which is a key part of my personality, drives me to dig deeper. If I see a post claiming something dramatic or too good to be true, I'll take a few minutes to search for more information from reliable news outlets or fact-checking websites. Living in Philadelphia, I'm surrounded by diverse perspectives, which reminds me that not everyone sees things the same way, and that helps me stay open-minded but cautious. My hobbies like gardening and baking have taught me patience and attention to detail—skills that translate well to spotting fake content. I don't rush to share or react; instead, I take my time to verify. Being married with a child, I'm also mindful of the impact fake content can have on families and communities, so I make an effort to report obvious misinformation when I see it. Ultimately, my approach is about balance: staying informed without getting overwhelmed, using common sense, and encouraging others in my network to do the same. It's not about being paranoid, but about being responsible in a digital world where fake content can spread as fast as a well-tuned engine.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: Age 40 suggests maturity; occupation as mechanic implies practical, evidence-based thinking; Philadelphia location indicates urban awareness; time on social media (1 hour daily) shows moderate engagement; psychological feature 'curious' drives verification; hobbies (gardening, knitting, baking) suggest patience and detail-orientation; OCEAN traits (high openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, low neuroticism) support balanced, proactive, social approach; married with children adds family responsibility angle; education and parents' background (mixed levels) inform critical thinking without overcomplication; overall tone is practical, calm, and community-focused, avoiding extremes.
response_parsed.tone: Practical, calm, community-focused, evidence-based, moderately engaged
